content:
I was testing something locally, and I noticed I'm getting these errors when cross-compiling for arm64.

aliceryhl@aliceryhl-l:~/rust-for-linux$ git checkout v6.11-rc1
aliceryhl@aliceryhl-l:~/rust-for-linux$ make LLVM=1 CLIPPY=1 ARCH=arm64
  UPD     include/config/kernel.release
  UPD     include/generated/utsrelease.h
  CALL    scripts/checksyscalls.sh
  BINDGEN rust/bindings/bindings_helpers_generated.rs
  CLIPPY L rust/bindings.o
  EXPORTS rust/exports_bindings_generated.h
  CLIPPY L rust/kernel.o
  EXPORTS rust/exports_kernel_generated.h
  CC      init/version.o
  AR      init/built-in.a
  CC      kernel/sys.o
  AR      kernel/built-in.a
  CC      rust/helpers.o
  CC      rust/exports.o
  AR      rust/built-in.a
  AR      built-in.a
  AR      vmlinux.a
  LD      vmlinux.o
  OBJCOPY modules.builtin.modinfo
  GEN     modules.builtin
  MODPOST vmlinux.symvers
  UPD     include/generated/utsversion.h
  CC      init/version-timestamp.o
  LD      vmlinux
ld.lld: error: undefined symbol: __extendsfdf2
>>> referenced by core.a6f5fc5794e7b7b3-cgu.0
>>>               rust/core.o:(<f32>::midpoint) in archive vmlinux.a
>>> referenced by core.a6f5fc5794e7b7b3-cgu.0
>>>               rust/core.o:(<f32>::midpoint) in archive vmlinux.a

ld.lld: error: undefined symbol: __truncdfsf2
>>> referenced by core.a6f5fc5794e7b7b3-cgu.0
>>>               rust/core.o:(<f32>::midpoint) in archive vmlinux.a
make[2]: *** [scripts/Makefile.vmlinux:34: vmlinux] Error 1
make[1]: *** [/home/aliceryhl/rust-for-linux/Makefile:1156: vmlinux] Error 2
make: *** [Makefile:224: __sub-make] Error 2
Here's the config: https://gist.github.com/Darksonn/74b71df1258a2ee1e03b87b4ed400f64
If I open menuconfig and turn off rust, then it builds.
@Christian Schrefl did the arm32 support I think
Sorry I totally read that wrong, this thread isn't 32-bit
We shouldn't be linking any float symbols in the final binary right? They definitely exist in core but it seems like they should be fine unresolved

What we currently do is provide a weak symbol that panics. It is not great at all. First of all, because we manually list the symbols, which isn't great. But we should not even have those calls being generated to begin with (unless really some code needs them -- very little code needs it, and it is compiled differently in the C side).

Ideally, we would have a compiler flag to disable all that where not needed (possibly even not accepting code referring to f32 etc.). I think @Gary Guo took a look at some point but, while disabling the formatting bits was easy, the rest would not be so much.

Still, it would be nice to get rid of the hack we have. Or at least reduce it a bit (e.g. can we avoid listing them one by one?).

It is not the first time I see <f32>::midpoint being the cause...

Also, with rustc 1.82.0-nightly (64ebd39da 2024-08-03), I also got a couple more on top:

ld.lld: error: undefined symbol: __eqdf2
>>> referenced by core.20495ea57a9f069d-cgu.0
>>>               rust/core.o:(<f64>::next_up) in archive vmlinux.a
>>> referenced by core.20495ea57a9f069d-cgu.0
>>>               rust/core.o:(<f64>::next_down) in archive vmlinux.a
I thought we added a check in rustc to catch unintended fp intrinsics

So this depends on the rustc version? It looks like my rustc 1.78.0 builds my 6.11.0-rc1-arm64 vmlinux nicely:

$ rustc --version
rustc 1.78.0 (9b00956e5 2024-04-29)
$ strings vmlinux | grep "Linux version"
Linux version 6.11.0-rc1-arm64.....
It is likely due to the CONFIG_CC_OPTIMIZE_FOR_SIZE=y in her config.
(The fact that I see one more than her for the same config may have to do with the compiler, yeah, I used a nightly)

Gary Guo said:

I thought we added a check in rustc to catch unintended fp intrinsics

In the compiler? Hmm... Could you please remind me?
I double checked there's only a test that checks no_fp_fmt_parse exists
Maybe we should add a check, perhaps by compiling against softfp and link against compile-rt with hardfp to see if no symbols are unresolved?
Usually the fix on rustc side is to mark those functions as #[inline]
All of {midpoint,next_up,next_down} are indeed unstable functions not marked as inline...
@Miguel Ojeda Perhaps we should discuss this for our next meeting with lang team
Added a few bits at: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/514
And linked to here.

Miguel Ojeda said:

It is likely due to the CONFIG_CC_OPTIMIZE_FOR_SIZE=y in her config.

Ok, I confirm that I have NOT set that.
It would be great to have some build time error when calls to the float operations are generated. This would remove the need for generating panicking intrinsics (at least for float ops).
Well, we do this by leave symbols undefined
But overtime we have to add these back because there're new functions in libcore that call these

But this currently just always errors as the float ops are used in the core library.
What I meant was allow using core without float ops so that we don't have to define them,
Ideally we would not need any panicing intrinsics.

Looks like Miguel posted a fix:
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20240806150619.192882-1-ojeda@kernel.org/
Should we update the Rust CI setup so it would have caught this problem?
It depends on how many compute they can give us, and we want to add first a few other things, but I think it would be a good idea to have the -Os build there.
i.e. we would need to go up to linking, which it is not done now, so that is a fairly big commitment.
Maybe there should be a test that you can build core without linking floating point support, with the few known stubs, then work toward making it build without any stubs
Right, that could probably be its own test.
There are similar "special builds for core" tests like the ones we have for no_fp_fmt_parse and no_global_oom_handling, so perhaps they would be OK with that.
i.e. those are already in the Rust repository.
